subtopic: Prevention of thromboembolism in valvular AF
clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman with rheumatic mitral stenosis presents for a routine cardiology review. Echocardiography shows a mitral valve area of 1.2 cm², left atrial diameter of 4.9 cm, and moderate mitral regurgitation. Her ECG confirms atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm. She has no prior stroke or TIA. Her blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg and renal function is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate antithrombotic strategy for this patient?
options: Aspirin 75 mg daily
Warfarin with target INR 2.0–3.0
Apixaban 5 mg twice daily
Rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily
Clopidogrel 75 mg daily
explanation: Atrial fibrillation in the setting of rheumatic mitral stenosis (moderate-to-severe) carries a high embolic risk and is a Class I indication for anticoagulation with vitamin K antagonists (warfarin, target INR 2.0–3.0). Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are specifically contraindicated in rheumatic mitral stenosis because they have not been shown to be non-inferior to warfarin in this setting (the INVICTUS trial showed rivaroxaban was inferior). Antiplatelet therapy alone is inadequate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Aspirin alone is insufficient for stroke prevention in AF with rheumatic mitral stenosis; antiplatelet agents do not adequately prevent cardioembolic events from this substrate.
Correct. Warfarin (target INR 2.0–3.0) is the guideline-recommended anticoagulant for AF in rheumatic mitral stenosis; DOACs are contraindicated in this specific valve pathology.
DOACs including apixaban are contraindicated in moderate-to-severe rheumatic mitral stenosis; the INVICTUS trial demonstrated inferiority of rivaroxaban in this condition.
Rivaroxaban is a DOAC and is specifically contraindicated in rheumatic mitral stenosis with AF; trials have confirmed inferiority compared to warfarin.
Clopidogrel as monotherapy provides no clinically meaningful protection against cardioembolic stroke in valvular AF and is not guideline-supported here.
clinicalPearl: DOACs are contraindicated in rheumatic mitral stenosis with AF — warfarin remains the only evidence-based anticoagulant in this setting.
commonExamTrap: Choosing a DOAC (apixaban or rivaroxaban) because the patient has non-valvular AF — rheumatic MS is classified as 'valvular' AF, where DOACs are contraindicated.
missedConcept: The distinction between valvular (rheumatic MS, mechanical valves) and non-valvular AF is critical for anticoagulant choice.

topic: Aortic stenosis — medical management and intervention threshold
clinicalStem: A 74-year-old man with known calcific aortic stenosis presents with a 3-month history of exertional dyspnoea and two syncopal episodes on exertion. Examination reveals a slow-rising pulse, a harsh ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids, and a soft S2. Echocardiography shows an aortic valve area of 0.7 cm², mean gradient 52 mmHg, and preserved left ventricular ejection fraction of 58%. His creatinine is 120 µmol/L and he has no significant coronary disease on CT coronary angiogram. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Commence amlodipine to reduce afterload
Commence spironolactone and furosemide for symptom control
Refer for transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) or surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR)
Commence digoxin and increase follow-up to every 6 months
Commence statin therapy to slow valve calcification progression
explanation: This patient has symptomatic severe aortic stenosis (AVA 0.7 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg) with exertional syncope and dyspnoea — both are class I indications for aortic valve replacement. Valve replacement (TAVI or SAVR depending on surgical risk) is the only definitive treatment; there is no effective pharmacological therapy to delay progression once symptoms appear. Vasodilators such as amlodipine can cause dangerous hypotension in fixed outflow obstruction.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vasodilators (amlodipine, ACE inhibitors) are relatively contraindicated in symptomatic severe AS because fixed outflow obstruction prevents compensatory cardiac output increase, risking severe hypotension.
Loop diuretics may temporarily palliate dyspnoea but do not address the underlying fixed obstruction; they are not definitive and can reduce preload dangerously in AS.
Correct. Symptomatic severe AS (AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg, symptoms of syncope/dyspnoea/angina) is a Class I indication for valve replacement — TAVI or SAVR based on surgical risk score.

Digoxin has no role in isolated aortic stenosis with preserved EF; watchful waiting is inappropriate once the patient is symptomatic — prognosis without intervention is poor (50% mortality at 2 years).
Statins do not slow progression of calcific aortic stenosis — the SEAS and SALTIRE trials failed to show benefit; statin use is not indicated solely for this purpose.
clinicalPearl: Symptomatic severe AS (syncope, angina, dyspnoea) is a cardiac emergency requiring urgent valve replacement — median survival without intervention is <2 years.
keyClue: Exertional syncope + harsh ejection systolic murmur + AVA 0.7 cm² + mean gradient 52 mmHg
commonExamTrap: Starting medical therapy (diuretics, ACE inhibitors) instead of referring for valve replacement once AS becomes symptomatic.
missedConcept: There is no effective medical therapy for severe symptomatic AS; the only treatment is mechanical relief of obstruction.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man with a history of rheumatic fever in childhood has moderate mitral regurgitation on echocardiography. He requires an elective dental extraction under local anaesthetic. He has no drug allergies. Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding antibiotic prophylaxis for infective endocarditis in this patient?
options: Prophylaxis is mandatory; amoxicillin 3 g orally 1 hour before the procedure is recommended
Prophylaxis is not routinely recommended; maintaining good oral hygiene is the most important preventive measure
Prophylaxis is indicated; clindamycin 600 mg IV is the agent of choice
Prophylaxis is mandatory; ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally 1 hour prior is recommended
Prophylaxis is only indicated if the patient has had a previous episode of infective endocarditis
explanation: Current ESC and NICE guidelines no longer recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures in patients with acquired valvular disease (such as rheumatic mitral regurgitation) who do not fall into the highest-risk category. The highest-risk group (requiring prophylaxis) includes patients with prosthetic valves, previous IE, cyanotic congenital heart disease, or repaired congenital defects with residual shunts. Good oral hygiene is emphasised as the most effective preventive strategy for all cardiac patients. The old regimen of amoxicillin 3 g is no longer recommended in most guidelines.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amoxicillin 3 g was the old British regimen; current ESC/NICE guidelines do not recommend routine prophylaxis for rheumatic mitral regurgitation without other high-risk features. The dose used when prophylaxis IS given is amoxicillin 2 g (or 3 g in some guidelines) — but the primary issue is this patient is not in the highest-risk group.

Correct. Current guidelines restrict IE prophylaxis to the highest-risk patients (prosthetic valves, previous IE, cyanotic CHD, repaired CHD with residual shunts); rheumatic MR alone does not qualify. Oral hygiene maintenance is the cornerstone of prevention.
Clindamycin is used in penicillin-allergic high-risk patients; it is not the agent of choice and this patient is not in the high-risk prophylaxis group.
Ciprofloxacin is not recommended for IE prophylaxis; it is not an appropriate agent for this indication regardless of risk stratification.

Previous IE is one of the highest-risk indications, but prophylaxis applies to other categories (prosthetic valves, certain congenital defects) too; however, rheumatic MR alone does not qualify even without prior IE.
clinicalPearl: IE prophylaxis is now restricted to the highest-risk patients (prosthetic valves, previous IE, cyanotic CHD); rheumatic valvular disease alone no longer qualifies in most current guidelines.
keyClue: Rheumatic mitral regurgitation — no prosthetic valve, no prior IE, no cyanotic CHD
commonExamTrap: Assuming any structural heart disease mandates IE prophylaxis — current guidelines have significantly narrowed the indication.
missedConcept: The paradigm shift from broad to targeted IE prophylaxis, and the primacy of good oral hygiene in prevention.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old intravenous drug user presents with a 3-week history of fever, night sweats, and malaise. Examination reveals temperature 38.9°C, a new pan-systolic murmur at the left sternal edge, and stigmata of IE (splinter haemorrhages, Janeway lesions). Blood cultures are taken. Transoesophageal echocardiography confirms a 12 mm vegetation on the tricuspid valve. Blood cultures subsequently grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?
options: Vancomycin IV for 6 weeks
Flucloxacillin IV for 4–6 weeks
Amoxicillin IV plus gentamicin IV for 4 weeks
Ceftriaxone IV for 2 weeks
Doxycycline oral for 6 weeks
explanation: MSSA native valve endocarditis is treated with flucloxacillin (an anti-staphylococcal penicillin) IV for 4–6 weeks. Flucloxacillin achieves superior bactericidal activity against MSSA compared to vancomycin, which should be reserved for MRSA or penicillin-allergic patients. Right-sided (tricuspid) IE in IVDU may sometimes be treated for a shorter duration (2 weeks) with specific regimens, but standard therapy remains flucloxacillin for 4–6 weeks. Vancomycin is only appropriate for MRSA or confirmed penicillin allergy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vancomycin is inferior to flucloxacillin for MSSA IE and is associated with worse clinical outcomes; it should only be used for MRSA or in penicillin-allergic patients — not as first-line for MSSA.

Correct. Flucloxacillin IV for 4–6 weeks is the first-line treatment for MSSA native valve endocarditis per ESC guidelines; it provides superior bactericidal activity over vancomycin for susceptible strains.
Amoxicillin plus gentamicin is the regimen for streptococcal endocarditis (e.g., viridans streptococci); it is not appropriate for staphylococcal IE.
Ceftriaxone is appropriate for streptococcal or HACEK organism endocarditis, not for Staphylococcus aureus which requires bactericidal anti-staphylococcal therapy.
Oral doxycycline is not appropriate for active infective endocarditis with vegetations; IV bactericidal therapy is mandatory for this life-threatening infection.
clinicalPearl: For MSSA endocarditis, flucloxacillin is superior to vancomycin — reserve vancomycin for MRSA or penicillin allergy.
keyClue: MSSA on blood cultures + tricuspid valve vegetation + IVDU
commonExamTrap: Defaulting to vancomycin for all staphylococcal IE — vancomycin is only preferred when MRSA is confirmed or penicillin allergy exists.
missedConcept: Anti-staphylococcal penicillins (flucloxacillin) are bactericidal and clinically superior to vancomycin for MSSA.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old man presents with sharp chest pain that is worse on inspiration and leaning forward, and relieved by sitting up. He had a viral upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks ago. Examination reveals a pericardial friction rub. ECG shows saddle-shaped ST elevation in all leads with PR depression. Troponin is borderline elevated at 0.06 ng/mL (upper limit of normal 0.04). Echocardiography shows a small pericardial effusion with normal LV function. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily plus colchicine 0.5 mg twice daily for 3 months
Prednisolone 40 mg daily for 4 weeks
Aspirin 300 mg three times daily alone for 2 weeks
Metoprolol 50 mg twice daily and bed rest
Intravenous methylprednisolone for 3 days
explanation: Current ESC guidelines recommend NSAIDs (ibuprofen or aspirin) plus colchicine as the first-line treatment for acute pericarditis. Adding colchicine to NSAIDs significantly reduces the risk of recurrence (from approximately 30% to 15%). Treatment is given for 1–3 months with tapering. Corticosteroids are second-line, reserved for NSAID failure or contraindication, as they are associated with increased recurrence rates when used as first-line therapy. The mildly elevated troponin indicates myopericarditis, which does not change the initial treatment approach.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Ibuprofen (or aspirin) plus colchicine is the evidence-based first-line regimen for acute pericarditis; colchicine added to NSAIDs halves the recurrence rate (COPE and ICAP trials).

Corticosteroids are not first-line for acute pericarditis; they increase recurrence risk and are reserved for cases refractory to NSAIDs/colchicine or specific indications (autoimmune, uraemic pericarditis).
Aspirin alone without colchicine is suboptimal — guidelines consistently recommend combination therapy (NSAID + colchicine) to reduce recurrence; monotherapy with aspirin is inferior.
Metoprolol has no role in treating pericarditis; while rest is advised, beta-blockade does not treat the underlying inflammatory process.
IV methylprednisolone is reserved for severe or refractory cases and carries the risk of increasing recurrence; it is not appropriate as initial therapy for straightforward acute pericarditis.
clinicalPearl: Always add colchicine to NSAIDs in acute pericarditis — it halves recurrence risk and is supported by randomised trials (COPE, ICAP).
keyClue: Saddle-shaped ST elevation + PR depression + post-viral + pericardial friction rub
commonExamTrap: Starting corticosteroids as first-line treatment — steroids increase recurrence and are only second-line in pericarditis.
missedConcept: Colchicine is a mandatory add-on to NSAIDs in acute pericarditis to prevent recurrence, not an optional adjunct.

clinicalStem: A 66-year-old woman with known metastatic breast cancer presents to the emergency department with severe progressive dyspnoea and hypotension. She is confused and diaphoretic. BP is 75/50 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, JVP markedly elevated, and heart sounds are distant. Her neck veins paradoxically distend during inspiration. ECG shows electrical alternans and sinus tachycardia. A bedside echocardiogram confirms a large circumferential pericardial effusion with right ventricular diastolic collapse. What is the most urgent intervention?
options: Start IV fluid bolus 500 mL and urgent cardiology referral
Urgent pericardiocentesis
Start dobutamine infusion for cardiogenic shock
Insert a chest drain for pleural effusion
Start furosemide 80 mg IV for right heart failure
explanation: This patient has cardiac tamponade — a life-threatening emergency characterised by Beck's triad (hypotension, elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds), pulsus paradoxus (paradoxical JVP elevation = Kussmaul's sign), electrical alternans, and echocardiographic RV diastolic collapse. Urgent pericardiocentesis is the definitive and immediate treatment. Fluids can temporarily support BP while pericardiocentesis is being set up, but relief of mechanical obstruction is the priority. Diuretics are absolutely contraindicated as they reduce preload and worsen haemodynamic compromise.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV fluids can be used as a short-term temporising measure to maintain preload while preparing for pericardiocentesis, but they are not the definitive treatment and do not address the mechanical cause of shock.
Correct. Urgent pericardiocentesis is the definitive treatment for cardiac tamponade; removal of as little as 50–100 mL of pericardial fluid can produce dramatic haemodynamic improvement.

Dobutamine does not relieve the mechanical obstruction of tamponade and does not address the underlying cause; it will not improve cardiac output when the heart cannot fill due to external compression.
This is pericardial effusion causing tamponade, not a pleural effusion — a chest drain placed in the pleural space will not relieve pericardial compression.
Furosemide is absolutely contraindicated in tamponade; reducing preload in a patient who is critically preload-dependent will cause cardiovascular collapse.
clinicalPearl: In cardiac tamponade, furosemide and vasodilators are lethal — the heart is critically preload-dependent; only pericardiocentesis relieves the obstruction.
keyClue: Electrical alternans + RV diastolic collapse on echo + Beck's triad + metastatic cancer
commonExamTrap: Giving furosemide for 'raised JVP' without recognising tamponade — this will cause cardiovascular collapse in a preload-dependent state.
missedConcept: Cardiac tamponade is a mechanical emergency requiring mechanical relief (pericardiocentesis), not pharmacological management.
redFlagReason: Cardiac tamponade causes obstructive shock and cardiovascular arrest if not immediately treated with pericardiocentesis; diuretics are contraindicated and fatal.

subtopic: Interpretation of heart murmurs in clinical context
clinicalStem: A 67-year-old woman is referred for assessment of a cardiac murmur. She has a history of rheumatic fever in her 20s and presents with increasing fatigue and exertional dyspnoea over 6 months. On examination: pulse is irregularly irregular, BP 110/85 mmHg, and JVP is elevated at 4 cm above the sternal angle. Auscultation reveals a loud S1, an opening snap after S2 followed by a low-pitched rumbling mid-diastolic murmur at the apex, best heard in the left lateral decubitus position. Which valvular pathology is most consistent with these findings?
options: Aortic regurgitation
Mitral regurgitation
Mitral stenosis
Tricuspid stenosis
Pulmonary stenosis
explanation: The findings are classic for mitral stenosis: loud S1, opening snap (OS) in early diastole, and a low-pitched rumbling mid-diastolic murmur best heard at the apex in the left lateral decubitus position. The OS-S2 interval narrows as stenosis severity increases (louder S1, shorter S2-OS interval = more severe). The associated atrial fibrillation, elevated JVP, and wide pulse pressure are consistent with haemodynamic consequences of MS. Rheumatic fever is the predominant cause globally.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Aortic regurgitation produces an early diastolic decrescendo murmur at the left sternal edge (not apex), a wide pulse pressure, and collapsing pulse — not an opening snap or rumble.
Mitral regurgitation causes a pan-systolic murmur radiating to the axilla with a soft S1 (not loud) — no opening snap; the murmur is systolic, not diastolic.
Correct. Mitral stenosis produces the classic triad of loud S1, opening snap, and mid-diastolic rumble at the apex; atrial fibrillation is a common complication.
Tricuspid stenosis is rare, produces a diastolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge (not apex), increases with inspiration, and is usually accompanied by prominent 'a' waves in JVP.
Pulmonary stenosis produces an ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge with a click; it is not a diastolic murmur and is not associated with rheumatic fever.
clinicalPearl: Mitral stenosis: loud S1 + opening snap + mid-diastolic rumble at apex in left lateral decubitus — best heard with the bell, not the diaphragm.
keyClue: Opening snap + mid-diastolic rumble at apex + loud S1 + irregularly irregular pulse + rheumatic fever history
commonExamTrap: Confusing the opening snap of MS with S3 — OS occurs earlier (after S2), is high-pitched, and is best heard at the apex with the diaphragm.
missedConcept: The opening snap in mitral stenosis is produced by sudden tensing of the fused mitral leaflets at the end of rapid ventricular filling.

subtopic: Medical management of chronic severe AR
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with known chronic severe aortic regurgitation due to a bicuspid aortic valve is reviewed. He has mild exertional dyspnoea but denies syncope or chest pain. Echocardiography shows an LVEF of 52%, LV end-systolic dimension of 51 mm, and severe AR. Blood pressure is 160/55 mmHg (wide pulse pressure). He is currently taking no cardiac medications. Surgery has been discussed but deferred by the patient. Which pharmacological strategy is most appropriate at this time?
options: Start bisoprolol to reduce heart rate and improve diastolic filling
Start nifedipine or ACE inhibitor to reduce afterload and delay surgery
Start spironolactone to prevent LV remodelling
No pharmacological therapy is indicated; observation alone is appropriate
Start digoxin to maintain cardiac output
explanation: In chronic severe aortic regurgitation with systolic hypertension (wide pulse pressure), vasodilators (ACE inhibitors or long-acting dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers such as nifedipine) reduce afterload, decrease regurgitant volume, and may slow progression of LV dilation. This is particularly relevant when surgery is declined or must be deferred. Guidelines support vasodilators when systemic hypertension is present. The patient here has LV dysfunction (EF 52% is borderline) and hypertension, making afterload reduction appropriate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Beta-blockers reduce heart rate and prolong diastole, which may worsen AR severity by increasing the time available for regurgitation; they are generally avoided as primary therapy for AR.
Correct. Vasodilators (ACE inhibitors, long-acting CCBs) reduce afterload and regurgitant fraction in severe AR with hypertension, helping to preserve LV function and delay surgical intervention.
Spironolactone is an aldosterone antagonist used in heart failure with reduced EF; it is not a standard therapy for isolated chronic severe AR without overt heart failure.

Observation alone is insufficient given hypertension (BP 160/55), borderline LVEF (52%), and dilated LV (LVEDD); pharmacological management is indicated to reduce afterload and prevent further LV deterioration.
Digoxin has no established role in chronic AR with preserved rhythm; it does not address the underlying haemodynamic burden of volume overload.
clinicalPearl: In severe AR with hypertension, ACE inhibitors or dihydropyridine CCBs reduce afterload and regurgitant volume — avoid beta-blockers as they slow heart rate and worsen regurgitation.
keyClue: Chronic severe AR + wide pulse pressure (160/55) + borderline LVEF + patient declines surgery
commonExamTrap: Prescribing beta-blockers for 'heart rate control' in AR — slower heart rate prolongs diastole and increases regurgitant time, worsening AR.
missedConcept: In AR, the regurgitant fraction is influenced by heart rate (slower HR = more regurgitation) and afterload — vasodilators reduce both.

topic: Infective endocarditis — Duke criteria and diagnosis
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man with a mechanical mitral valve replacement presents with 3 weeks of fever, malaise, and weight loss. On examination, temperature is 38.6°C, he has tender nodules on his fingertips (Osler nodes), and a new regurgitant murmur is heard. Two sets of blood cultures grow viridans streptococci. Transthoracic echocardiography is inconclusive. Transoesophageal echocardiography (TOE) shows a 9 mm vegetation on the prosthetic valve with no perivalvular abscess. According to the modified Duke criteria, what is this patient's diagnostic status?
options: Rejected IE (non-cardiac source identified)
Possible IE (1 major criterion only)
Definite IE (2 major criteria)
Definite IE by pathological criteria only
Possible IE (1 major + 1 minor criterion)
explanation: The modified Duke criteria classify IE as definite if 2 major criteria are met. Major criterion 1: typical microorganism (viridans streptococci) from 2 separate blood cultures. Major criterion 2: positive echocardiography — vegetation on a prosthetic valve visualised on TOE. Both major criteria are fulfilled, making this definite IE (2 major). The clinical features (Osler nodes = minor criterion) confirm the diagnosis, but 2 major criteria alone suffice for definite classification.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is clearly active infective endocarditis with two major Duke criteria met; rejection would require an alternative diagnosis explaining all features or resolution within 4 days of antibiotics.
Possible IE requires 1 major + 1 minor OR 3 minor criteria; this patient meets 2 major criteria, qualifying for definite IE, not possible.
Correct. Two major Duke criteria are met: (1) typical organism in 2+ blood cultures; (2) vegetation on prosthetic valve on TOE — this constitutes definite IE.
Pathological criteria require either positive histology from vegetation/embolic fragment or positive culture of vegetation — surgical or post-mortem material has not been obtained here.
This patient meets 2 major criteria, not 1 major + 1 minor; Osler nodes are a minor criterion but are not needed to establish the diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Two major Duke criteria = Definite IE: (1) typical organism in ≥2 blood cultures + (2) positive echo (vegetation, abscess, or new prosthetic valve dehiscence).
keyClue: Viridans streptococci in 2 blood cultures + vegetation on prosthetic valve on TOE = 2 major Duke criteria
commonExamTrap: Counting Osler nodes as a major criterion — they are a minor criterion; only microbiological and echocardiographic findings constitute major criteria.
missedConcept: The modified Duke criteria require 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria for definite IE classification.

subtopic: Sport restriction and pharmacotherapy in acute myocarditis
clinicalStem: A 22-year-old competitive marathon runner presents with 5 days of fatigue, chest discomfort, and palpitations following a bout of COVID-19 infection 2 weeks ago. ECG shows widespread T-wave inversions and occasional PVCs. Troponin T is elevated at 0.48 ng/mL. Cardiac MRI shows subepicardial/mid-myocardial late gadolinium enhancement in a non-ischaemic distribution, confirming acute myocarditis. LVEF is 48% on echocardiography. In addition to standard heart failure therapy, which of the following is the most important management recommendation?
options: Immediate return to training is safe if symptoms resolve within 48 hours
Mandatory rest from competitive sport for at least 3–6 months with re-evaluation before return
Start high-dose methylprednisolone immediately for 4 weeks
Refer for ICD implantation immediately given the elevated troponin
Prescribe ivermectin as antiviral therapy to reduce myocardial inflammation
explanation: In acute myocarditis, competitive sport and strenuous exercise must be restricted for at least 3–6 months regardless of symptom resolution. This is because exercise during active myocarditis dramatically increases the risk of ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death. Return to sport requires resolution of symptoms, normalisation of cardiac biomarkers, LV function, and ideally CMR evidence of resolved inflammation. The mildly reduced LVEF (48%) and non-ischaemic CMR pattern confirm myocarditis. Immunosuppression is not routinely indicated and should only be considered in giant cell or specific autoimmune myocarditis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Return to sport within 48 hours is dangerous; exercise in acute myocarditis is associated with ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death — the myocardium is electrically unstable during active inflammation.
Correct. ESC guidelines mandate a minimum 3–6 month restriction from competitive sport in acute myocarditis, with return only after clinical, biomarker, functional, and imaging criteria are met.

Corticosteroids are not routinely indicated for acute viral myocarditis; they are reserved for specific histological subtypes (giant cell myocarditis, eosinophilic myocarditis, or autoimmune myocarditis refractory to standard therapy).

ICD is not immediately indicated; primary prevention ICD criteria require sustained arrhythmia assessment after recovery; premature ICD implantation in recoverable myocarditis may expose the patient to unnecessary procedural risk.
Ivermectin has no evidence-based role in COVID-19 myocarditis or any myocarditis; it is not part of any guideline recommendation.
clinicalPearl: Acute myocarditis requires ≥3 months of sport restriction — exercise during active myocarditis carries a risk of sudden cardiac death from ventricular arrhythmias.
keyClue: Competitive athlete + post-viral CMR-confirmed myocarditis + PVCs + reduced LVEF
commonExamTrap: Recommending immediate immunosuppression for all myocarditis — corticosteroids are only indicated for specific subtypes (giant cell, eosinophilic, autoimmune).
missedConcept: Exercise restriction is as therapeutically important as pharmacotherapy in myocarditis — physical activity during inflammation is the leading modifiable risk factor for SCD in young athletes.

topic: Mitral valve prolapse — screening and management
clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman is referred after an incidental finding of a mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic murmur on routine examination. She is asymptomatic and runs regularly. Her 24-year-old brother was recently diagnosed with mitral valve prolapse (MVP). Echocardiography confirms classic MVP with leaflet displacement >2 mm above the annular plane, mild mitral regurgitation, and normal LV dimensions and function. Regarding screening of her first-degree relatives and her own long-term management, which statement is most accurate?
options: First-degree relatives do not require screening as MVP is not heritable
She should immediately undergo prophylactic mitral valve repair before developing significant regurgitation
First-degree relatives should be offered echocardiographic screening; she should be followed with serial echocardiography and can continue exercise with reassurance
She requires antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures due to her MVP diagnosis
She should be started on metoprolol to prevent arrhythmias associated with MVP
explanation: Mitral valve prolapse has an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance (FLNC, MYH7 gene mutations); first-degree relatives have approximately a 50% risk and should undergo echocardiographic screening. This asymptomatic patient with mild MR and normal LV dimensions and function should be managed with surveillance echocardiography (every 3–5 years for mild MR) and may continue exercise. MVP alone (without high-risk features) no longer qualifies for IE prophylaxis under current guidelines. Prophylactic surgery is not indicated in asymptomatic mild MR.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MVP does have a heritable component — autosomal dominant with variable penetrance; familial forms are well-described, making first-degree relative screening important.
Prophylactic surgical repair is not indicated for asymptomatic mild MR; surgical intervention is reserved for severe MR with symptoms or evidence of LV dysfunction/dilation or new-onset AF.

Correct. Family screening by echocardiography is recommended given the hereditary nature; serial surveillance is appropriate for mild MR with normal LV, and exercise need not be restricted in low-risk asymptomatic MVP.

Current ESC and NICE guidelines do not recommend IE prophylaxis for MVP (even with mild MR) unless the patient is in the highest-risk category (previous IE, prosthetic valve, cyanotic CHD) — isolated MVP no longer qualifies.
Beta-blockers are sometimes used for symptomatic palpitations or chest pain in MVP syndrome, but routine prophylactic beta-blockade is not recommended for asymptomatic patients with only mild MR.
clinicalPearl: MVP has a heritable basis — screen first-degree relatives with echocardiography; isolated MVP without high-risk features no longer qualifies for IE prophylaxis.
keyClue: Mid-systolic click + late systolic murmur + family history of MVP + leaflet displacement >2 mm
commonExamTrap: Prescribing IE antibiotic prophylaxis for MVP — this is no longer recommended unless the patient is in the highest-risk IE category.
missedConcept: MVP can be familial (autosomal dominant); family screening is a clinical obligation, and most patients with mild isolated MVP require only surveillance.

topic: Cardiovascular risk factor screening — hypertension detection
subtopic: White coat hypertension and ambulatory blood pressure monitoring
clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man is referred by his GP after three clinic BP readings averaging 148/92 mmHg over 4 weeks. He is asymptomatic, a non-smoker, and has no family history of premature cardiovascular disease. Examination is otherwise unremarkable with no end-organ damage. His fasting glucose, cholesterol, and renal function are normal. ECG is normal. To confirm the diagnosis before committing to lifelong antihypertensive therapy, which investigation is most appropriate?
options: Arrange repeat clinic BP measurement in 1 year
Start amlodipine 5 mg immediately based on three elevated readings
Arrange 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM)
Request renal artery Doppler ultrasound to exclude secondary hypertension
Arrange an exercise stress test to unmask hypertensive response
explanation: Current ESC/NICE hypertension guidelines recommend 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) or home blood pressure monitoring (HBPM) to confirm hypertension before initiating treatment in patients with clinic readings in the Stage 1 range (140–159/90–99 mmHg). This is to exclude white coat hypertension (present in 20–30% of patients with elevated clinic readings) and masked hypertension. ABPM is the gold standard and provides superior prognostic information. Treatment should be commenced once ABPM confirms a daytime average of ≥135/85 mmHg.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A 1-year delay without confirmation is inappropriate; ABPM should be done promptly to either confirm hypertension (requiring treatment) or identify white coat hypertension (requiring surveillance, not medication).
Starting antihypertensive therapy based solely on clinic readings without ABPM/HBPM confirmation risks treating white coat hypertension unnecessarily with lifelong medication.
Correct. ABPM is the recommended gold-standard test to confirm hypertension before committing to treatment, allowing exclusion of white coat effect and providing 24-hour cardiovascular risk data.

Renal artery Doppler is appropriate for investigating suspected secondary hypertension (resistant hypertension, young patient, asymmetric renal size, renal bruit); this patient has no features to suggest secondary cause.
Exercise stress testing is used to investigate coronary artery disease or exercise-induced arrhythmias; it is not indicated for confirmation of hypertension diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Always confirm hypertension with ABPM or HBPM before starting lifelong therapy — 20–30% of patients with elevated clinic readings have white coat hypertension.
keyClue: Stage 1 clinic BP elevation (148/92) with no end-organ damage, no secondary cause features — needs ABPM to confirm before treating
commonExamTrap: Starting antihypertensives based on clinic readings alone without ABPM confirmation — this may result in unnecessary long-term treatment of white coat hypertension.
missedConcept: ABPM is the diagnostic gold standard for hypertension confirmation; clinic readings alone are insufficient to mandate treatment in borderline cases.
